text: new her, but ended up alternating between scalding showers of manic rage and prolonged naps of overwhelming depression. She had broken more than she’d fixed. Erin knew she was sinking but feared the stigma of being seen as a victim, so she sought help in another town. The lights of the elevator flickered overhead and Erin clearly remembered Doctor Mason’s office, with its smell of citrus essential oils and the supple texture of leather furniture. Caught somewhere between a ’70s cliché and new age therapy, it had turned out to be exactly what Erin needed. Erin’s reactions were welcomed and worked through with a soft voice and kind smile. There were exercises and prescriptions—treating her highs and lows until she finally evened out. And when her emotions were balanced, the doctor suggested flooding to break through what had become an almost obsessive fear, a phobia—the parking garage at work. Her breath caught at the idea of the airy four-story garage, and Erin reached over to steady herself on the elevator’s handrail. I’ve come too far to fail here. Erin inhaled through her nose and exhaled out her mouth, releasing the air slowly through pursed lips. She remembered the work she’d done, the progress she’d made. In the nose, out the mouth, three more times as she tried to remember her healing rather than her trauma. Once her allotted time-off had been depleted, Erin returned to work but not her parking spot. Rather than using the attached garage, available to employees and customers of the York Towers office building, Erin parked in the lot down the block. She then walked through the front door of the office building to ride the elevator to the fourth floor. Rain or shine, and through several frigid winter days, she’d gone the extra mile to not use the parking garage. Doctor Mason began breaking down that wall the moment Erin had revealed her new habit. “Flooding is a technique in which you immerse yourself in your fear in a safe way.” The doctor had explained the behavior therapy procedure. “Considering your situation, we can do it in stages. First things first, we have to get you out of the cold.” Erin moved her car to the ground floor of the garage, but parked as close to the entrance as possible and all but running back out to the safety of the sidewalk rather than walking through the garage itself. She then continued to the front door of the building as she had been, but was proud of her baby step of actually being within the cement prison, even if only briefly. After a month, the doctor pushed Erin to the next step, her parking space. But Erin couldn’t do it. She couldn’t be on that floor. Erin imagined little things she had walked past every day without noticing and expected they would jump out and scream her location, remind her of exactly where she’d been when it had happened. Erin settled for the second level of the parking garage, the floor with the skywalk, knowing it was a hub and should be occupied at all times. She made sure to arrive and leave when the bulk of the building’s employees did, so she wouldn’t be walking alone through the echoing tomb to and from the skywalk. Her knuckles whitened as Erin gripped the railing in the elevator, eyes aimed at the floor but focused in the past. She felt the weight of depression creeping in as she relived months of therapy, but fought the tendrils of fear threatening to drag her down. She inhaled deeply, counted to three, and exhaled. I can do this. Erin remembered the hard work of processing and conquering her anger, even as she dug inside to find it again and tap it for strength, telling herself, Just a little. Erin had been successfully using the second level of the parking garage for a little over a month and had almost returned to her pre-attack emotional indifference to the space within the cement walls. Parking next to Gloria had been an accident the first time, but the woman was a familiar face to Erin and brought comfort. The owner-operator of the second floor Starbucks, Gloria was the perfect companion to walk beside, and Erin quickly made it a habit. The coffee nook was positioned beside the elevators, and Erin had begun to convince herself their twice-daily meetings were a friendship rather than conveniently linked to the garage or flooding of her fear. Gloria. Erin pushed the button marked L2 and the doors opened again immediately—not called elsewhere by other passengers, the elevator had simply waited for her to decide where to go. She poked her head out. The Starbucks was dark. “Shit.” Erin hadn’t meant to stay so late. She’d lost track of time, working diligently with her project spread out on the long conference room table. If she’d been at her own desk, near windows, she would have noticed the setting sun. She would have known to leave. Now the building was all but empty and promised the same of its attached parking garage. I can do this. She swallowed hard, exhaled shakily, and stepped from the elevator. Her pounding heart felt as if it was going to break through her ribs, and Erin put a hand to her chest as if to prevent its escape. She jumped as the elevator doors hissed closed behind her. And though Erin knew she could push the button and go back in, she held her ground, resolute in the decision to continue. Retreat would be failure. Acutely aware of her surroundings, Erin walked toward the exterior metal door, which led to the skywalk and the garage beyond. She had only gone alone into the garage twice, and both times had been midday, under a bright sun. Not at night. Never at night. She paused in front of the Starbucks. Why didn’t Gloria wait for me?
